title: Passionflower tincture for mild anxiety
subtitle: A 4-6 week folk-method herbal tincture
excerpt: Dried passionflower aerial parts soaked in 40% vodka for 4-6 weeks. A nerve-calming herb herbal tincture traditionally used for mild anxiety and difficulty unwinding. Long soaking draws the flavonoids and alkaloids into the spirit.
sourceNotes: King's American Dispensatory (Felter & Lloyd, 1898), Passiflora incarnata: this spirit extraction preparation and nerve-calming herb use. the herbal reference on Passiflorae herba, the well-established evidence for mild anxiety and sleep support, this spirit extraction dose range.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Macerate for 4-6 weeks in a cool dark cupboard, shaking daily. Strain and bottle when ready; the finished herbal tincture keeps 2 years in a dark glass bottle, cool and dark.

projectSchedule.title: Pack the jar and cover with vodka
projectSchedule.body: Layer the dried passionflower in the soaking jar. Pour over the vodka to cover by at least 2 cm. Seal, label, and shake once. Place in a cool dark cupboard.

projectSchedule.title: Check and top up if needed
projectSchedule.body: Shake the jar well. If the herb has absorbed the spirit and is exposed above the liquid, top up with a little more vodka to keep it covered.

projectSchedule.title: Strain and bottle this spirit extraction
projectSchedule.body: Strain this spirit extraction through muslin, pressing the spent herb firmly. The finished herbal tincture should be deep green-gold. Bottle in amber glass with the date.

glossaryTerms.definition: A liquid extraction of herb in alcohol and water. The spirit draws out a wider range of constituents than water alone, and preserves the preparation for 2 years or more.

The traditional folk tincture method: dried herb packed into a jar and covered with 40% spirit (vodka) by volume. Simpler than the weight-to-volume pharmacopoeial method but produces a reliable preparation for home use.

content.text: is among the simplest preparations for mild anxiety in the western nerve-calming herb tradition. Dried aerial parts packed into a jar at a ratio of approximately 1 part herb to 5 parts 40% vodka by weight, sealed and left to soaked for 4-6 weeks. The spirit draws the flavonoids and alkaloids out of the herb; the finished herbal spirit extraction is dosed at 2-4 ml in a little water, taken up to three times a day or specifically at times of acute stress. The preparation keeps 2 years in amber glass.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Sedatives. Passionflower potentiates benzodiazepines, barbiturates, antihistamines, sleeping tablets, and other CNS depressants. Do not combine with any sedating medicine without consulting your prescriber.

Driving. Passionflower can cause drowsiness. Do not drive after taking this preparation, especially on first use.

Pregnancy. Passionflower is avoided in pregnancy due to traditional concerns. Consult a herbalist or midwife before use in pregnancy.

Alcohol content. The herbal spirit extraction contains approximately 40% alcohol by volume at the stated dose (2-4 ml per dose). This is a very small amount of alcohol in total (about 0.8-1.6 ml per dose). If you must avoid alcohol, use a glycerite or infusion instead.

This preparation is for mild, situational anxiety. Clinical anxiety, panic disorder, or depression should be assessed by a doctor or mental health professional. Passionflower is not a replacement for prescribed medication.

items.prepNote: dried Passiflora incarnata aerial parts (leaf, stem, flower); not ornamental garden passionflower species
plain vodka, 40% ABV; the 40% spirit is the reference standard for folk tinctures

content.text: Pack the dried passionflower loosely into a 500 ml soaking jar. Pour over the vodka slowly; the herb will absorb it quickly at first. The vodka should cover the herb by at least 2 cm. Seal the jar with a tight-fitting lid. Label it with the herb, the vodka strength, and the date. Shake well and place in a cool, dark cupboard.

Shake the jar once a day, every day. This moves the herbs through the spirit and prevents channelling. At 2 weeks, check that the herb is still submerged; if it has swollen and risen above the spirit, press it down with a clean spoon and add a little more vodka to cover. At 4 weeks, the spirit should be deep green-gold and smell strongly of the herb. 6 weeks gives the deepest extraction.

Line a sieve with muslin cloth folded double. Set it over a measuring jug. Pour this spirit extraction slowly through. Gather the muslin corners and press the spent herb firmly to extract all the liquid (this is where a significant portion of this spirit extraction sits). You should end up with approximately 200-250 ml of deep green-gold herbal spirit extraction. Decant into amber dropper bottles using a small funnel. Label each bottle with the herb, the date, and the dilution ratio (1:5, 40% spirit).

Adults: 2-4 ml in a small glass of water, up to three times a day. For sleep support, take a single 3-4 ml dose 30-60 minutes before bed. Take for up to 4 weeks continuously; take a 1-week break before another course. Not for children under twelve. Not for those who should avoid alcohol.
Amber glass bottles in a cool, dark cupboard. The finished herbal spirit extraction keeps 2 years from the bottling date. If this spirit extraction looks cloudy, smells off, or tastes wrong, discard.
When not to use this remedy

On any sedating medication. In pregnancy. When driving the same day. Clinical anxiety or depression needs professional assessment. If anxiety is accompanied by chest pain, palpitations, breathlessness, or is severely affecting daily life, see your doctor.
